articles.title: The Free Market Lie: Why Switzerland Has 25 Gbit Internet and America Doesn't
articles.summary: Switzerland's 25 Gbit internet access is often cited as the fastest dedicated residential connection in the world due to its highly regulated telecom sector with strong oversight and government-backed infrastructure projects. In contrast, the United States has limited fiber service options, often shared with neighbors and having only one choice of provider, leading to stagnation and monopolies. Germany's similar situation, where fiber is limited to one provider and often shared, highlights the difference between a deregulated market in Switzerland and a heavily regulated one in Germany. This disparity suggests that free markets can lead to hyper-competition and world-leading speeds, but also come with problems such as monopolies and inferior infrastructure.

articles.title: 14× faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
articles.summary: The authors of the article are rebuilding the ONNX path for embeddings in Manticore, which is now faster than the previous SentenceTransformers/Candle path by 14 times on average. This improvement comes from optimizing the backend to run on multiple cores and reducing latency in concurrent calls. The new path uses a single-threaded worker with parallelized operations inside the call, resulting in significant speed gains for bulk ingest throughput.

articles.title: Arbitrary code execution breaking sandboxes in KDE Plasma
articles.summary: Arbitrary code execution breaking sandboxes in KDE Plasma demonstrates how malicious applications can impersonate and spawn arbitrary binaries on the host when user invokes Open New Window action. This is achieved by exploiting vulnerabilities in Flatpak applications, which are sandboxed environments that run within a Linux container. The exploit uses the `meson` compiler to build an evil binary that can be installed into a Flatpak app without proper permission, allowing it to impersonate and launch arbitrary code on the host.

articles.title: An American Privacy Emergency
articles.summary: Quantum computers won't solve hard problems instantly by just trying all solutions in parallel, a concept that has been proven true for years, and will be effectively stifled by a recent Trump administration action that would ban modern techniques for preserving privacy in large datasets such as the 2030 US Census. Advances in confidentiality provision had enabled the Census Bureau to share increasing quantities of data at more granular detail, but the order issued on June 4, 2026, will result in less useful statistics and weaker protection. The acting force behind this order is political interest rather than scientific merit, bypassing legally required administrative procedures and reflecting both the rhetoric and misunderstandings of representatives of the Center for Renewing America.

Institutional credibility can also be used sparingly and with caution, as it may not situate the individual in a specific context. To effectively ask for help, one must first understand what is going on and then provide a concise explanation of their context that connects to things they already know.
